lesson.menu_title: Negotiating with Confidence -- Standing Your Ground
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 -- Negotiating with Confidence -- Standing Your Ground
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Natalie Viegas | Aula 19 -- Negotiating with Confidence

listenings.text: Hi Natalie, Tom here, just before your call with the client tomorrow. A quick word on standing your ground. When they push back on the price, do not cave in right away. Hold the line, but stay calm and professional. The trick is to set clear conditions. Say something like, we can be flexible on the timeline, as long as the volume stays the same. Or, unless we sign a longer contract, we cannot lower the rate. That way you stay firm without sounding rigid. And always give your rationale. Do not just say no, justify it with one solid reason. Remember, some points are non-negotiable, so know them before you walk in. You have leeway on the small things, but stand firm on what really matters. Be assertive, not aggressive. You will do great. Talk tomorrow.

Hi, this is Natalie, recording a quick note after my negotiation with the client. I was nervous, but I followed Tom's advice. When they pushed back hard on the price, I did not cave in. I held the line, but I stayed calm. The part that worked best was setting conditions. I said we could move on the timeline, as long as the volume stayed the same. And I made it clear that, unless they committed to a longer contract, we could not lower the rate. I gave my rationale for every point, so it never sounded like a flat no. There were two things that were non-negotiable, and I stood firm on those. But I gave them some leeway on the smaller details, and that built trust. I have learned that being assertive is not the same as being aggressive. It is about staying firm, justifying your position, and making the deal worthwhile for both sides. We reached an agreement, and I felt confident the whole time.

extra_audio.text: First, you listen to the pushback calmly and do not cave in right away. Then you give your rationale, so your position never sounds like a flat no. After that, you set a clear condition, using as long as or unless, to show where you can be flexible. Next, you stand firm on the points that are non-negotiable, but you offer some leeway on the smaller details. Finally, you confirm the terms and make the deal worthwhile for both sides.
